entries.title: Decoder overview - encoding, decoding, and hashing data
entries.body:
# Burp Decoder - overview

**Decoder** is Burp's workbench for transforming data: it encodes, decodes, and hashes arbitrary strings and bytes in common formats. If you came across something unreadable in the traffic - a Base64 string, a URL-escaped parameter, a hex dump, a gzip body - Decoder brings it to a human-readable form and back. This is a local tool: it sends nothing to the server, it simply churns the data on your machine.

## Why it is needed in practice

In the web, data is constantly encoded and repackaged: parameters in the URL are percent-escaped, tokens and cookies often sit in Base64, binary data is represented in hex, response bodies are gzip-compressed. To understand what is inside a value, and all the more to **replace** it with your payload in the correct encoding, you must be able to take that value apart and put it back together. That is exactly what Decoder exists for.

Typical tasks:

* **Read a token/cookie.** You see `eyJ1c2VyIjoiZ3Vlc3QifQ` in a cookie - ran it through Base64 and saw `{"user":"guest"}`. From there it is clear what to change.
* **Assemble a payload in the right encoding.** For an injection to reach the application intact, special characters often have to be URL-encoded (`'` -> `%27`), sometimes twice. Decoder does this in one click.
* **Take nested encodings apart.** Real values are not infrequently encoded in several layers (for example, URL on top of Base64). Decoder unwinds them one by one.
* **Quickly compute a hash.** You need to compare a value with the MD5/SHA of something - you compute it right here.

## Supported formats

Decoder can encode and decode in a set of classic web formats:

* **URL** - percent encoding, `%XX`.
* **HTML** - HTML entities (`<`, `'`, etc.).
* **Base64** - the most common case for tokens, cookies, `Authorization` headers.
* **ASCII hex** - the hexadecimal representation of characters.
* **Hex**, **Octal**, **Binary** - numeric representations of bytes.
* **GZIP** - decompressing/compressing gzip data (useful for compressed bodies).

Separately there is a **Hash** function - to compute the hash of data. The set of available algorithms depends on what the Java platform in use provides, but these are the familiar **MD5**, **SHA-1**, **SHA-256**, etc. families.

## Decoder's four actions

All the work revolves around four operations, available in the drop-down lists opposite each data panel:

1. **Decode as** - decode the data in the selected format (for example, "read this as Base64").
2. **Encode as** - encode the data in the selected format (for example, "URL-encode this").
3. **Hash** - apply a hash function and get a digest.
4. **Smart decode** - "smart" automatic decoding: Burp tries to recognize the format itself and strip the encoding, in several layers if needed.

## Text and Hex

Any data panel can be viewed in two modes - **Text** and **Hex**. Text mode is convenient for readable strings; hexadecimal (**Hex**) is indispensable when the data has non-printable bytes (for example, after gzip decompression or decoding a binary token) - there you see the exact values of each byte.

## Editions

Decoder is in both **Community Edition** and **Professional** - it is a basic tool, available in both editions without restrictions.

## Quick start

```
1. Open the Decoder tab.
2. Paste data into the top field (or send it from the traffic:
   right-click a request/response > Send to Decoder).
3. To the right of the field choose an action, for example Decode as > Base64.
4. Below, a new panel will appear with the result - the already decoded value.
5. You can apply another action to the result (see the section on
   multi-layer transformations).
```

Further in this section we will examine in detail the manual mode with a stack of panels, Smart decode, and hashing.

entries.title: Manual mode, multi-layer transformations, and Smart decode
entries.body:
# Manual mode, multi-layer transformations, and Smart decode

Decoder's main feature is the **stacked panels**. The result of each transformation opens in a new panel below, and you can immediately apply the next action to it. This way the data is unwound (or wound up) layer by layer, and each step is visible separately.

## How to load data

There are three ways to put data into Decoder:

* **Manually** - just type or paste a string into the top field.
* **From the traffic** - right-click a request or response in any Burp tool (Proxy, Repeater, Logger, etc.) and choose **Send to Decoder**. The whole message lands in the top panel.
* **Partial selection** - select only the needed fragment in the message (for example, a single cookie value) and send/copy exactly it to Decoder. This is handy so as not to fuss with the extra.

## How the stack of panels works

The logic is simple and intuitive:

1. In the top panel - the source data.
2. Opposite the panel you choose an action (**Decode as / Encode as / Hash / Smart decode**) and a format.
3. Burp creates a **new panel below** with the result of that transformation.
4. The new panel has **its own** set of actions. You apply the next transformation to the already-obtained result.
5. And so on as many times as needed: the chain of panels records the entire path from the source data to the final form.

This is exactly the multi-layer (nested) transformation. An example of unwinding a token encoded as URL-on-top-of-Base64:

```
Panel 1 (source):  %7B%22role%22%3A%22user%22%7D
   -> Decode as > URL
Panel 2:           {"role":"user"}
```

And now the reverse scenario - assemble a payload for an injection that must be handed over URL-encoded:

```
Panel 1:  ' OR '1'='1
   -> Encode as > URL
Panel 2:  %27%20OR%20%271%27%3D%271
   -> Encode as > URL   (second iteration - for double URL encoding)
Panel 3:  %2527%2520OR%2520%25271%2527%253D%25271
```

Double URL encoding (as in the example above) is a working technique when a value passes through two layers of decoding along the way (for example, the web server decodes once, the application decodes again), and you need the special character to "survive" all the way to the target point.

## Smart decode - "smart" decoding

When you do not know what format the data is in, there is no need to guess manually. Click **Smart decode** - Burp applies heuristics, recognizes a fragment that looks like a familiar format, and strips the encoding itself. If under one layer there turned out to be another (URL on top of Base64), Smart decode will try to strip that too, laying the result out across panels.

An important caveat: Smart decode works **by guesswork (heuristically)** and therefore **can make mistakes**. For example, a string that happens to look like hex or Base64 it may "decode" the wrong way. Therefore:

* use Smart decode for quick reconnaissance of "what even is this";
* if the result looks like garbage - do not trust it and switch to **manual** mode, explicitly specifying the needed format via **Decode as**;
* remember that you always see the whole chain of panels and can rewind any step.

## Color highlighting

Decoder highlights transformations with color, helping you visually distinguish which exact type of encoding/decoding was applied at each layer. This is especially valuable in a long stack of panels: by the color you immediately see where Smart decode took something for Base64 and where for URL, and whether it went off track.

## Practice

* **Unwind cookies and tokens from top to bottom.** A suspicious value -> Smart decode -> if you recognized a layer (for example, Base64) -> read the contents -> if needed strip the next one.
* **Prepare the payload in Decoder, but send it from Repeater/Intruder.** A convenient flow: you assembled a correctly encoded string in Decoder, copied it, pasted it into the right position of the request in Repeater.
* **Do not forget about Hex mode** for binary results: after gzip decompression or decoding a binary token the text view is useless, whereas Hex will show the real bytes.
* **Double-encoding is a frequent filter bypass.** If single URL encoding of the payload did not work, try running **Encode as > URL** twice: sometimes that is exactly how the injection gets through normalization on the server.

entries.title: Hashing data in Decoder
entries.body:
# Hashing data in Decoder

Besides encoding and decoding, Decoder can compute **hashes**. This is handy when you need to quickly get the digest of a string and not leave Burp - for example, to check a value against a hash that the application uses as a token, identifier, or checksum.

## How to compute a hash

1. Put the data into a Decoder panel (typed in manually or sent via **Send to Decoder**).
2. Opposite the panel, in the drop-down list choose the **Hash** action.
3. Specify the needed algorithm from the list.
4. The result - the digest - will appear in a new panel below.

Like any other action, hashing fits into the **stack of panels**: you can first decode something and then compute the hash of the already-decoded result, and vice versa.

## Which algorithms are available

The set of available hash functions **depends on the Java platform** on which Burp is running: Decoder shows the algorithms the environment provides. In practice these are the familiar families:

* **MD5** - 128-bit, long since cryptographically broken, but still ubiquitous in applications.
* **SHA-1** - 160-bit, also considered insecure for protection, but widely used.
* **SHA-2** (**SHA-256**, **SHA-384**, **SHA-512**) - the current family.

The specific list may differ slightly depending on the Java version, but the algorithms listed are available almost always.

## Why this is needed in testing

* **Crack the structure of a token.** Often an "incomprehensible" value in an application is a hash of something predictable: an e-mail, a user ID, a time, an incremental counter. The hypothesis is easy to test: compute, say, the `MD5` of the suspected value and compare it with what the application returns. It matched - you understood how the token is generated and may be able to forge it.
* **Prepare the expected value.** If a request parameter is a hash of other fields (a naive "signature" of the form `hash(secret + data)` or just `hash(data)`), recompute it for your data so the request passes the check.
* **Verify checksums.** Quickly confirm that two values are the hash of one and the same input.

## A small practical scenario

Suppose that in a password-reset link the token looks like 32 hex characters. The hypothesis is that it is `MD5`. We check:

```
1. We take the assumed input (for example, the victim's e-mail or their user id).
2. Send to Decoder -> Hash -> MD5.
3. We compare the resulting digest with the token from the link.
4. Matched? Then the token is predictable -> you can generate
   a valid reset link for any account. This is a serious finding.
```

## Practice and caveats

* **Hash functions in Decoder are without salt and without a secret.** Decoder computes the "plain" hash of data. If the application mixes in a salt or a secret key (as in HMAC), you will not get a simple match - and that, by the way, is a good sign that the generation is done properly.
* **Decode first, then hash (if needed).** When the input is itself encoded, build a chain: Decode as -> then Hash, to hash exactly the source bytes rather than their encoded representation.
* **For serious hash cracking, use specialized tools.** Decoder is great for a one-off check of the hypothesis "is this the MD5 of X?". But if you need to find a preimage by a dictionary brute-force - that is a job for specialized utilities, not for Decoder.
